content:
3 min read
Just now

--

From research papers to cloud deployment, from debugging for hours to publishing real solutions — this is the story of my journey into Artificial Intelligence.

There is a common misconception that becoming an AI engineer starts with mastering every algorithm, completing countless certifications, or earning a prestigious degree.
My experience has been completely different.
It started with curiosity.
As a Computer Science student, I wasn’t satisfied with simply understanding concepts for examinations. Every new technology made me ask the same question:
“Can I build something useful with this?”
That single question shaped everything that followed.
The Beginning
My first few projects were far from perfect.
I encountered deployment failures, broken APIs, database issues, models that refused to converge, cloud services that denied permissions, and enough debugging sessions to question every career decision I had made.
There were days when a single missing dependency consumed hours.
There were nights when a model finally worked after hundreds of failed experiments.
Looking back, those failures taught me far more than success ever could.
Building Beyond Tutorials
Instead of stopping after online courses, I started building complete systems.
Some of the projects I’ve worked on include:

• AI-powered healthcare applications

• Earthquake forecasting using Machine Learning

• Climate and COVID-19 prediction using ensemble deep learning

• Payment gateway applications

• Inventory Management Systems

• API Monitoring and Anomaly Detection platforms

• Cloud-native applications deployed using AWS services

• Data visualization dashboards

• GIS and Digital Elevation Model analysis using MATLAB and TopoToolbox
Every project introduced a completely different challenge.
Some demanded strong machine learning knowledge.
Others required backend engineering.
Many forced me to learn cloud architecture, deployment pipelines, authentication, databases, and scalable system design.
Research Changed My Perspective
One of the most rewarding experiences has been working on research.
Unlike classroom assignments, research doesn’t come with an answer key.
You spend weeks reading papers.
You compare existing methods.
You question every assumption.
You redesign experiments.
Sometimes you discover that your “new idea” has already been published five years ago.
Sometimes you find a genuine improvement.
That process taught me something incredibly valuable:
Innovation isn’t about having a brilliant idea overnight.
It’s about asking better questions than yesterday.
Learning Cloud the Hard Way
Cloud computing looked easy in tutorials.
Reality was very different.
Permissions failed.
Deployments broke.
Instances stopped responding.
Security groups blocked traffic.
Models worked locally but crashed in production.
Those challenges forced me to understand not only how cloud services work but why they exist.
Today, I enjoy solving deployment problems almost as much as building machine learning models.
My Favorite Part: Solving Problems
I’ve realized something important.
I don’t enjoy coding for the sake of writing code.
I enjoy solving difficult problems.
Sometimes the solution involves Python.
Sometimes AWS.
Sometimes MATLAB.
Sometimes SQL.
Sometimes it requires reading dozens of research papers before writing a single line of code.
Technology changes.
Problem-solving stays.
What Comes Next?
This is only the beginning.
I’m currently exploring:

• Artificial Intelligence

• Machine Learning

• Data Science

• Deep Learning

• Cloud Computing

• MLOps

• Research Engineering

• Distributed Systems
My goal isn’t simply to build projects.
It’s to build systems that create real impact.
Why I’m Writing on Medium
For a long time, I believed I should wait until I became an expert before sharing what I knew.
I was wrong.
Knowledge grows when it’s shared.
Through this blog, I’ll document:

• My AI projects

• Research experiences

• AWS deployment lessons

• Machine Learning experiments

• Debugging stories

• Project architectures

• Technical tutorials

• Career lessons for students entering AI and Data Science
If even one article helps someone avoid a mistake that took me days to solve, writing it will have been worthwhile.
Final Thoughts
Every project begins with uncertainty.
Every researcher starts with questions.
Every engineer spends hours debugging.
The difference isn’t talent.
It’s the willingness to keep building after things break.
This is my journey.
And this is only Chapter One.
If you’re also learning, building, researching, or simply curious about AI and technology, I’d love to connect.
Let’s learn together.
~Abhijit Ranjan
